claims.text: Current research assessment relies too heavily on output-based proxies such as papers, journal placement, citation metrics, h-indexes, REF-style outputs, author status, and institutional prestige, even though these proxies were historically more aligned with visible signals of productive research.

sources.title: San Francisco Declaration on Research Assessment
sources.notes: Used for reforming research assessment beyond journal impact and publication status.

sources.title: Agreement on Reforming Research Assessment
sources.notes: Used for diverse outputs, qualitative judgement, and responsible use of quantitative indicators.

sources.title: Bibliometrics: The Leiden Manifesto for research metrics
sources.authors: Diana Hicks, Paul Wouters, Ludo Waltman, Sarah de Rijcke, Ismael Rafols
sources.notes: Used for the critique of metric-led research evaluation.

sources.title: The Hong Kong Principles for assessing researchers: Fostering research integrity
sources.authors: David Moher, Lex Bouter, Sabine Kleinert, Paul Glasziou, Mai Har Sham, Virginia Barbour, and others
sources.notes: Used for rewarding responsible practices, transparency, open science, and diverse contributions.

sources.title: REF 2029 Open Access Policy
sources.notes: Used for REF-style output policy and the continued importance of output-attached governance.

claims.text: AI systems are increasingly able to draft, code, review, search, analyse, and repackage research outputs, so the reliability of polished output alone declines as an assessment signal.

sources.title: An AI Tsunami is about to Hit Science

claims.text: Falling execution costs intensify an existing mismatch between modular workflow-based research production and a bloated quantity-output assessment system that is not built to handle generative trends.

claims.text: Authorship remains a necessary responsibility category, but a scriptor differs from a prompter by treating the research workflow itself as an output of modular AI-mediated research production.

claims.text: Publication-based incentive structures should adapt to the rise of AI-generated papers by rewarding improvements to the warrant of claims, rather than rewarding output volume or encouraging strategic ambiguity and AI masking.

sources.title: Risks of AI scientists: prioritizing safeguarding over autonomy

sources.title: The Death of the Author
sources.notes: Used to clarify that the title invokes Barthes as a critique of authorial origin and interpretive authority, not as a claim that human responsibility disappears.

sources.title: Artificial intelligence and the death of the academic author
sources.notes: Used to situate the Barthes phrase in current academic AI authorship debate.

sources.title: Creating with AI: On recent debates about authorship revisiting the influence of Barthes and Foucault
sources.notes: Used for a current account of AI authorship debates revisiting Barthes and Foucault.

sources.title: Journal for AI Generated Papers
sources.notes: Used as a current example of a response to AI-generated papers that still organises the unit of account around submitted papers, screening, endorsement, AI review, peer review, and acceptance.
